lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors installing, replacing, repairing, and maintaining architectural glass, storefronts, interior glazing, and curtain-wall or related glazing systems for external customers, including firms whose recurring activity is a sequence of bid projects and service calls.
lens.excluded_activities: Automotive glass, prefabricated-window installation classified elsewhere, glass manufacturing, captive in-house crews, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and businesses lacking a transferable operating organization.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is primarily project-based or service-call contracting for general contractors, building owners, property managers, retailers, institutions, and homeowners; work is commonly won through fixed-price bids, negotiated subcontracts, unit pricing, or time-and-material repair orders.

a.rationale: Most wage cost is tied to physical measuring, material handling, cutting, rigging, installation, sealing, and repair at variable jobsites. AI exposure is concentrated in estimating and takeoff, bid drafting, submittals, procurement, scheduling, dispatch, invoicing, document search, and customer communication. The range is a wage-weighted judgment informed by the documented task mix, not a measured automation share.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted occupation table for NAICS 238150 paired with time-and-motion studies of estimators, project managers, dispatchers, and office staff using AI-assisted takeoff and document tools would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No current six-digit occupational wage mix was located for this industry.
O*NET and BLS describe occupations, not the share of contractor payroll exposed to AI.
Physical robotics at irregular jobsites is excluded unless implementable within five years.

rho.bridge: AGC/Sage measures technology intentions and constraints across construction contractors, not realized implementation among glazing contractors. I translate 44% expecting increased AI investment, 61% already using cloud project management, and 38% citing implementation/training time as a top IT challenge into a five-year implementation share centered near one-half. This likely overstates small glazing-firm readiness because the survey includes larger contractors, but may understate adoption induced by persistent hiring difficulty.
rho.rationale: Document-heavy office workflows can be adopted through existing software, but fragmented data, small-firm management bandwidth, field-to-office integration, quality review, and safety/accountability limit conversion of exposure into operating savings. Labor scarcity strengthens the incentive to implement tools that avoid incremental hiring.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort study of glazing contractors reporting AI-tool deployment, workflow-level usage, error rates, and verified labor hours avoided would replace the broad-construction proxy.
caveats: Broad construction respondents are not representative of the 74 estimated LMM glazing firms.
Investment intention is not completed implementation or labor substitution.
Implementation must preserve bid accuracy, code compliance, safety documentation, and accountable human review.

e.rationale: At the supplied $1-10M EBITDA scale, most firms should have employees, repeat external-customer contracting, and a transferable combination of crews, supplier relationships, estimating capability, equipment, and customer history. Eligibility is reduced for owner-dependent firms, highly concentrated project books, distressed operators, and businesses whose economics depend on nontransferable licenses or relationships.
e.change_evidence: A complete census of the 74 estimated LMM firms using contractor-license records, customer concentration, owner dependence, recurring-customer share, bonding history, and sale-readiness interviews would materially narrow this range.
caveats: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and estimated rather than observed at six digits.
The Census establishment count includes firms far outside the LMM EBITDA band and cannot measure eligibility.
Project revenue can recur at the customer level even when individual contracts are one-off.

s5.bridge: Gallup observed that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I discount that broad-employer intent rate because gifts, rare public offerings, internal family succession, abandoned plans, and non-control transactions do not all create a qualifying external control transfer. Construction-owner demographics and actual glazing deal completion could move the result in either direction.
s5.rationale: Employer businesses are materially more transfer-oriented than nonemployers, but intent is not completion and qualifying control sales are a subset of stated sell-or-transfer plans. The range therefore sits below the observed 22% broad-employer intention rate.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-transaction data from BizBuySell, GF Data, Axial, broker CRM systems, SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, and glazing-industry owner interviews linked to the estimated eligible-firm population would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses, not glazing contractors or the LMM EBITDA band.
Survey plans may not become completed transfers.
The estimate excludes closures, minority investments, internal reorganizations, and nontransferable owner-operator exits.

q.rationale: On fixed-price work, within-contract labor efficiencies can be retained until competitors rebid similar jobs; negotiated and time-and-material work passes more benefit through. Over five years, procurement sophistication, transparent bids, general-contractor pressure, and new entry should share a substantial portion with customers, while differentiation through response time, reliability, and capacity can preserve some benefit.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level gross-margin bridges by billing type, renewal or rebid price histories, win-loss data, and customer interviews quantifying demanded givebacks after documented productivity gains would materially change retention.
caveats: No glazing-specific evidence was found measuring pass-through of technology savings.
The mix of fixed-price, unit-price, and time-and-material work varies across commercial, residential, and repair contractors.
Demand volume effects and implementation costs are intentionally excluded from this primitive.

d5.bridge: BLS projects glazier employment, an occupation spanning multiple industries and including replacement demand, to rise 3% from 60,500 in 2024 to 62,500 in 2034. Converting the ten-year employment growth to roughly five years suggests about 1.5% growth; I round the base demand ratio to 1.02 and widen it for productivity, construction-cycle, and industry-mix uncertainty. Employment may understate demand if productivity rises and overstate it if labor scarcity constrains output.
d5.rationale: New construction plus repair and replacement of existing architectural glass support broadly stable real service quantity. Office weakness and construction cyclicality are offsets, while renovation, storefront, institutional, and specialized exterior work diversify demand.
d5.change_evidence: A glazing-specific constant-price revenue series split among new construction, replacement, repair, and maintenance, combined with five-year construction-put-in-place forecasts by end market, would materially change the range.
caveats: Occupational employment is not constant-price, constant-quality service demand.
The BLS forecast is national and ten-year rather than five-year and includes glaziers outside NAICS 238150.
The supplied compensation ratio uses 23815 and mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.

o.rationale: The service basket requires an accountable contractor to interpret site conditions, coordinate access and safety, handle heavy fragile materials, install and seal systems, and stand behind workmanship. Software can compress office labor and support field decisions but is unlikely to eliminate the responsible operating firm; factory preassembly can reduce some on-site quantity without replacing local installation and repair accountability.
o.change_evidence: Field evidence from modular facade suppliers, robotic glazing pilots, general contractors self-performing glazing, and contractor insurance or warranty structures showing measurable displacement of external glazing operators would materially change this share.
caveats: The range is judgmental and does not measure future robotics adoption.
Greater factory preassembly could shift value away from field contractors.
Self-service is more plausible for minor residential tasks than for commercial exterior systems.

narrative.executive_view: Glass and glazing contracting remains an operator-led physical service with modest AI opportunity concentrated in the office and coordination layer. The most plausible benefit is greater estimating, project-management, and administrative capacity rather than automation of glazier craft labor.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist quantity takeoff, specification and drawing search, bid drafting, submittal preparation, schedule updates, procurement comparisons, service dispatch, invoice coding, and customer communication. Humans still must validate scope and dimensions, manage exceptions, coordinate trades, and perform safety-critical installation and repair.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-price projects allow temporary retention of labor efficiencies, especially when speed and reliable execution relieve customer bottlenecks. Rebid competition and sophisticated general contractors should progressively pass a meaningful share of savings to customers, while time-and-material work passes through faster.
narrative.firm_availability: Most firms at the supplied LMM scale should be operating businesses with crews, systems, and relationships, but owner dependence and customer concentration reduce eligibility. Broad employer-owner survey evidence indicates a real transfer pool, though qualifying completed control sales should be materially below stated five-year transfer intentions.
narrative.demand_durability: Architectural glass installation and repair are tied to both new construction and the existing building stock. BLS expects glazier employment to grow modestly through 2034 and explicitly links demand to both construction and window repair or replacement, supporting a near-flat to modest-growth five-year quantity range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainties are the absence of a six-digit occupational wage mix, lack of glazing-specific AI outcome studies, construction cyclicality, office exposure, volatile material costs, bid error risk, and thin evidence on completed owner transfers. Factory preassembly and better design data may reduce field hours, while safety, warranty, and site variability constrain displacement.
narrative.principal_driver: AI-assisted estimating and project administration can expand throughput in a labor-constrained trade without attempting to replace physical glazing crews.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most compensation supports hands-on field work, so the wage-weighted implementable AI opportunity is limited and poorly measured.

sources.title: 238150: Glass and glazing contractors - Census Bureau Profile
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments installing glass panes and doing other building glass work, including new work, additions, alterations, maintenance, and repairs; reports 6,945 employer establishments for 2023.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Glaziers
sources.supports: Documents physical glazier duties, jobsite and injury conditions, 60,500 jobs in 2024, 62% employment in foundation/structure/building-exterior contractors, projected employment of 62,500 in 2034, and demand from both new construction and repair or replacement.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine Job Duties: Glaziers
sources.supports: Lists detailed physical and cognitive tasks including blueprint interpretation, measuring, estimating, cutting, rigging, installation, sealing, repair, material movement, and customer consultation.

sources.title: AGC/Sage 2025 Construction Hiring and Business Outlook
sources.supports: Reports 44% of construction firms anticipated increased AI investment, 61% used cloud project-management tools, and 38% cited time to implement and train on technology as a top IT challenge; also documents demand dispersion and project postponements.

sources.title: AGC/NCCER 2025 Craft Workforce Survey
sources.supports: Reports that 92% of nearly 1,400 construction firms had difficulty hiring for open positions, supporting the incentive to avoid incremental hiring.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, versus 9% of nonemployers, and distinguishes transfer plans from closure or no plan.
